coredata.dc:title: Invasive plants Spartina alterniflora-derived carbon quantum dots/copper-manganese phosphates for supercapacitor electrodes
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xueshi
He, Qingyi
Qi, Xiang
Wang, Xinyi
Hao, Yingshu
Zhang, Xianru
Zhang, Minchao
Song, Xinshan
Cao, Xin
coredata.dc:description: Spartina alterniflora, an invasive plant species, has caused significant ecological damage to coastal ecosystems. Traditional physical management methods generate substantial biomass waste, potentially leading to secondary environmental pollution. To address this, we propose a sustainable strategy to convert Spartina alterniflora into high-value carbon materials, mitigating environmental harm while enhancing the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor electrodes. Bimetallic phosphates, despite their high energy storage potential, face challenges such as large volume changes during cycling and poor kinetic performance. Carbon quantum dots, derived from Spartina alterniflora, offer advantages such as nanoscale dimensions, excellent photoelectric properties, high stability, and environmental compatibility, effectively addressing these limitations. The CMPCD-2 (Copper-manganese bimetallic phosphates doped with 2 mg of carbon quantum dots) composite demonstrated a specific capacitance of 277 F/g at 0.5 A/g. An asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device, constructed with the CMPCD-2 electrode as the positive electrode and activated carbon as the negative electrode, achieved an energy density of 17.8 Wh kg-1 at a power density of 2.1 kW kg-1. This work not only advances the development of bimetallic phosphate-based materials for energy storage but also provides a sustainable solution for the effective utilization of Spartina alterniflora, bridging ecological restoration and energy storage technology.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Spartina alterniflora
Bimetallic phosphate
Carbon quantum dots
Wide voltage range

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) SEM image of CMP-0, (c-d) CMP-0.25, (e-f) CMP-0.5, (g-h) CMP-0.75, (i-j) CMP-1; (Ⅰ-Ⅴ) SEM image of CMP-0.5.
(a-b) SEM image of CMPCD-1, (c-d) CMPCD-2, (e-f) CMPCD-3; (Ⅰ-Ⅵ) SEM image of CMPCD-2.
XRD patterns of (a) CMP-X and (b) CMPCD-Y, XPS spectrum of (c) CMP-X and (d) CMPCD-Y.

HRTEM images of (a) CMP-0, (b) CMP-0.25, (c) CMP-0.5, (d) CMP-0.75, (e) CMP-1, (f) CMPCD-1, (g) CMPCD-2, (h) CMPCD-3; SAED images of (Ⅰ) CMP-0, (Ⅱ) CMP-0.25, (Ⅲ) CMP-0.5, (Ⅳ) CMP-0.75, (Ⅴ) CMP-1, (Ⅵ) CMPCD-1, (Ⅶ) CMPCD-2, (Ⅷ) CMPCD-3.
(a-d) XPS spectra of Cu, Mn, O and P elements in CMP-0.5 sample, (e-i) XPS spectra of Cu, Mn, O, C, P elements in CMPCD-2 sample.

(a) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms curves of CMP-X samples, (b) N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms curves of CMPCD-Y samples, (C)CMP-X sample pore size distribution, (d)CMPCD-Y sample pore size distribution.

(a) CV curve of CMP-X samples, (b) CV curves of CMP-0.5 at different scanning speeds, (c) GCD curve of CMP-X samples, (d) GCD curves of CMP-0.5 at different scanning speeds, (e) the Nyquist plots of CMP-X, (f) log (peak current vs scan rate) of CMP-0.5.

(a) CV curve of CMPCD-Y samples, (b) CV curves of CMPCD-Y at different scanning speeds, (c) GCD curve of CMPCD-Y samples, (d) GCD curves of CMPCD-Y at different scanning speeds, (e) the Nyquist plots of CMPCD-Y, (f) log (peak current vs scan rate) of CMPCD-2.

(a) CV curve of CMPCD-Y samples, (b) CV curves of CMPCD-Y at different scanning speeds, (c) GCD curve of CMPCD-Y samples, (d) GCD curves of CMPCD-Y at different scanning speeds, (e) the Nyquist plots of CMPCD-Y, (f) Ragone plots of power and energy densities of asymmetric supercapacitors.
Comparison of specific capacitance with other electrode materials.
Rs and Rct for CMP and CMPCD.

head.ce:title: Invasive plants Spartina alterniflora-derived carbon quantum dots/copper-manganese phosphates for supercapacitor electrodes

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Spartina alterniflora, an invasive plant species, has caused significant ecological damage to coastal ecosystems. Traditional physical management methods generate substantial biomass waste, potentially leading to secondary environmental pollution. To address this, we propose a sustainable strategy to convert Spartina alterniflora into high-value carbon materials, mitigating environmental harm while enhancing the electrochemical performance of supercapacitor electrodes. Bimetallic phosphates, despite their high energy storage potential, face challenges such as large volume changes during cycling and poor kinetic performance. Carbon quantum dots, derived from Spartina alterniflora, offer advantages such as nanoscale dimensions, excellent photoelectric properties, high stability, and environmental compatibility, effectively addressing these limitations. The CMPCD-2 (Copper-manganese bimetallic phosphates doped with 2 mg of carbon quantum dots) composite demonstrated a specific capacitance of 277 F/g at 0.5 A/g. An asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) device, constructed with the CMPCD-2 electrode as the positive electrode and activated carbon as the negative electrode, achieved an energy density of 17.8 Wh kg-1 at a power density of 2.1 kW kg-1. This work not only advances the development of bimetallic phosphate-based materials for energy storage but also provides a sustainable solution for the effective utilization of Spartina alterniflora, bridging ecological restoration and energy storage technology.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the rapid growth of the global economy, the demand for fossil energy is increasing rapidly, but its inefficient utilization leads to increasingly serious environmental problems. This has prompted great research attention to the development of renewable energy storage devices, and electrochemical energy storage technology is widely recognized as one of the key ways to achieve this goal [1,2]. Conventional redox electrode materials, such as metal sulfides [3], metal oxides [4]and hydroxides [5], suffer from problems such as agglomeration, which reduces the performance of the electrodes. To solve the problems arising in the above materials, coupling biomass-based materials with conventional electrode materials is currently one of the most effective solutions to improve the electrochemical performance of supercapacitors. Biomass-based materials such as carbon nanotubes [6,7], graphene [8,9] and carbon quantum dots [10,11], effectively improve the energy storage performance of the electrode materials due to their wide source, low cost, large specific surface area and good conductivity. The development and utilization of biomass materials can simultaneously achieve environmental governance, supply clean energy and combat climate change, with multiple environmental and social benefits.

Spartina alterniflora (English name: smooth cordgrass, a perennial herbaceous plant in the family Poaceaeand genus Spartina.) is produced in the east coast of North America and the Gulf of Mexico. It was introduced into China in 1979 due to its alkali resistance, waterlogging resistance, strong fertility and developed root system. Since then, it has been used for beach protection, dike protection, silt promotion and land making for more than 20 years. In recent decades, the invasive species Spartina alterniflora has exhibited aggressive expansion along China's coastal regions, spreading from Liaoning Province in the northeast to Hainan Island in the south. This rapid proliferation has triggered significant ecological consequences, severely disrupting native coastal ecosystems through habitat encroachment and biodiversity reduction. The species' dense growth patterns have simultaneously impacted human economic activities, causing substantial losses to mariculture operations through oxygen depletion and waterway obstruction, while also impeding navigation channels and increasing maritime transportation costs. These dual environmental and socioeconomic impacts have positioned Spartina alterniflora as a major ecological management challenge in China's coastal zones. In order to address the serious impact of Spartina alterniflora on the environment and ecosystems in China, China has initiated a special action program for the prevention and control of Spartina alterniflora, with the aim of achieving effective management of Spartina alterniflora in China by 2025. Therefore, Spartina alterniflora becomes one of the ideal raw materials for the preparation of biomass-based materials. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), as a new type of carbon materials, have attracted much attention for their tiny size (only a few nanometers), low toxicity, good water dispersion and excellent electrical conductivity. CQDs have shown a wide range of applications in many fields, such as bioimaging, biosensing, photocatalysis, light-emitting diodes and photovoltaics [12,13]. Especially in the field of electrochemical energy storage, CQDs not only act as conductive agents, but also as structure-directing agents, thus providing new ideas for the performance optimization of electrode materials [12]. By converting Spartina alterniflora into carbon quantum dots, not only the efficient utilization of biomass resources can be achieved, but also the growing demand for carbon quantum dots in various fields can be met [14]. This green biosynthetic route not only solves the recycling problem of Spartina alterniflora, but also provides new possibilities for the development of clean energy and electrochemical energy storage technologies [15,16].

Transition metal phosphates are commonly used as electrode materials for supercapacitors [17]. Due to their high conductivity and the presence of both metallic and covalent bonds, which provide more electrons for charge transfer, as well as their easily adjustable structure, they offer a balance between energy and power density, with a high specific capacity and cycling stability. Common transition metal phosphates include manganese phosphates, manganese phosphates [18], nickel phosphates [19] and cobalt phosphates [20]. Yangyang Chen [21] combined carbon nanotubes and nickel-cobalt phosphate nanosheets with light weight carbonized wood as a substrate, and was able to have excellent energy density (39.5 Wh kg-1) at a high power density (12.1 Wh kg-1). Nan Zhang [19] synthesized carbon nanofibers (CNF) loaded with bimetallic phosphate (CoxNi1-xP) ultrafine nanocrystals were synthesized and the prepared flexible solid-state asymmetric supercapacitors provided an energy density of 32.2 Wh kg-1 at 3.5 kW kg −1 .

Manganese has a high theoretical specific capacitance due to its ability to exhibit different chemical valence states [22] (+2,+3,+4,+5,+6 and +7) during redox processes, which also means that they can store more charge in supercapacitors, thus increasing the energy density of supercapacitors [23]; It also has good electrochemical activity and is capable of rapid redox reactions on the electrode surface. This helps to improve the charging/discharging rate and cycle life of supercapacitors [24]. The element copper has good conductivity and electrical conductivity, and in supercapacitors [25], the electrodes need to have good electrical conductivity to ensure the rapid transfer of charge between the electrodes, so copper can be used as an electrode material, which helps to improve the performance of supercapacitors [26]. In summary, considering the abundance of manganese and copper, low cost, high specific capacitance, and good electrochemical properties, we choose to use copper-manganese bimetallic for the preparation of phosphides in this paper.

As a typical invasive species, its large-scale biomass resource utilization and ecological governance collaborative strategy has significant environmental benefits: through the green preparation process of carbon quantum dots (CDs), it not only effectively transforms biomass waste, but also fits the concept of sustainable chemical development. Based on the easy characteristics of copper manganese bimetal phosphate and CDs low cost preparation advantage of composite electrode material, relying on the copper manganese multivalent resynergy and CDs surface carboxyl / hydroxyl functional group of electrolyte ion adsorption-diffusion regulation mechanism, can significantly improve the super capacitor, at the same time reduce the cost of device manufacturing. This research provides a new idea for multidisciplinary intersection by integrating invasive plant treatment, nanocarbon material design and electrochemical energy storage technology.

This paper describes the progress of the performance study of Spartina alterniflora-derived carbon quantum dot-doped copper-manganese bimetallic phosphate (CMP) composites for asymmetric hybrid supercapacitor devices. Brown carbon quantum dots were obtained by hydrothermal method using citric acid and Spartina alterniflora, and CMPCD was obtained by adding the carbon quantum dots during the hydrothermal synthesis of copper-manganese bimetallic phosphates (CMPs), and the doping of the carbon quantum dots effectively improved the specific capacitance, which was 277 F/g using a three-electrode system in a 1 M KOH electrolyte operated at 0.5 A g-1 current density. In addition, ASC were established using CMPCD and AC as positive and negative electrodes, respectively, and the energy density of ASC was as high as 17.8 Wh kg-1 at a power density of 2.1 kW kg-1. This work demonstrates the promising potential of Spartina alterniflora-derived carbon doped copper-manganese bimetallic phosphates for electrochemical energy conversion and storage.

KOH, CuSO4∙5H2O, MnSO4, CO(NH2)2, KH2PO4, H2SO4, N-Methyl-2-Pyrrolidone (NMP), Carbon Black and Activated carbon were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) was purchased from Solvay (Shanghai) Co. All reagents are used as received without further purification.

Firstly, 5 mmol of CuSO4∙5H2O and 5 mmol of MnSO4 were dissolved in 30 mL of deionized water, named as solution A. At the same time, 20 mmol of CO(NH2)2 and 20 mmol of KH2PO4 were dispersed into 20 mL of deionized water, named as solution B. The solution A and the solution B were mixed uniformly, and then the mixed solution was poured into the polytetrafluoroethylene Teflon-lined autoclave, following by heating at 120 °C for 6 h in an oven. In order to study the effects of different molar ratios of copper and manganese in CuMnPO4 electrode materials on the electrochemical properties, the molar ratios of CuSO4–5H2O and MnSO4 were set as 0:1, 1:3, 1:1, 3:1 and 1:0, respectively, and the corresponding samples were named CMP-X (X is the molar ratio of copper ions in the activated material, 0, 0.25, 0.5, 0.75 and 1, respectively), the ratios of 1:0 and 0:1 correspond to single components, respectively, and are used to evaluate the individual roles and performance of each component in the material. On the other hand, ratios such as 3:1, 1:1, and 1:3 are employed to investigate the synergistic effects between the two components, aiming to determine whether an optimal ratio exists to enhance material performance. These ratios cover a wide range from single components to various mixed proportions, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of the impact of component variations on material properties. The experimental results can be used to validate theoretical hypotheses, such as whether specific phases or performance improvements emerge at certain ratios. Electrochemical performance tests were also conducted, including cyclic voltammetry (CV), constant current charge and discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dots doped copper-manganese bimetallic phosphates
ce:section.ce:para: Spartina alterniflora was cut into segments of ∼5 cm in length, washed with deionized water and dried in a drying oven at 60 °C. After all the surface water was removed, it was pulverized into powder by a pulverizer and passed through a 100-mesh sieve, and then placed in a dry and ventilated place for spare. 2 g of Spartina alterniflora powder and 1 g of citric acid were added to 50 ml of deionized water and stirred magnetically to make them mix evenly, and then transferred to 100 mL Teflon lined hydrothermal reactor, which was placed in an oven at 160 °C for 4 h. After cooling to room temperature, the liquid and solid products were separated by vacuum filtration. The filtrate containing Spartina alterniflora-derived carbon quantum dots (CDs) was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min and the supernatant was repeated until there was no obvious precipitation, and then the purified supernatant was dried under vacuum at 80 °C in a vacuum drying oven.

5 mmol of CuSO4∙5H2O, 5 mmol of MnSO4 and CDs powder of different mass (0,1,2, and 3 mg) were dissolved into a mixture of 30 mL of deionized water to form a transparent yellow-green color solution. which was then transferred to 100 mL of a hydrothermal reactor and reacted at 120 °C for 6 h. After 6 h of reaction, the hydrothermal reactor was cooled down naturally to room temperature, and the sample was dried and preserved. The sample was named as CMPCD-Y and then subjected to electrochemical tests.

The surface morphology of the synthesized materials was analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM; TESCAN MIRA LMS, Czech Republic) and other techniques. The crystalline phase structure of the samples was investigated using powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) (Rigaku Smart Lab SE, Japan, recorded with Cu Kα at 45 mA and 40 kV, λ = 1.504 Å). High resolution TEM were performed by using Thermo Fisher Talos F200X G2. Specific surface area and pore size were based on nitrogen adsorption/desorption data recorded by Micromeritics ASAP 2460, USA. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed in a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi+, USA. Electrochemical property measurements such as cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge (GCD), and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) measurements were recorded at room temperature using an electrochemical workstation (CHI660E). The Hg/HgO electrode and platinum flake electrode were used as the reference electrode and counter electrode, respectively.

The slurry was prepared by taking 80 wt % activated substrate, 10 wt % PVDF and 10 wt % conductive carbon black in NMP solvent. The mixture was thoroughly stirred and the resulting paste was applied coated onto clean carbon paper and dried overnight in the vacuum oven at 60 °C. The weight of the activated substrate was about 1 mg. In previous studies, it was found that there was a significant negative correlation between the electrochemical performance of the electrode material and its load, and the excessive activated material cannot fully participate in the electrochemical reaction, resulting in the reduction of material utilization. We determined that 1 mg was the optimal load, and this value allowed the electrode to exhibit optimal electrochemical performance. The copper-manganese bimetallic phosphate material is prone to decomposition under acidic conditions, rendering it unsuitable for testing in acidic electrolytes. In contrast, a 1 M KOH solution is highly compatible with a wide range of electrode materials, including carbon-based materials and metal oxides. The high mobility of K + and OH- ions in the solution contributes to enhanced ionic conductivity, while the electrolyte's stability across a broad potential window ensures reliable capacitor performance under high-voltage conditions. Additionally, the use of KOH electrolyte facilitates the improvement of the specific capacitance of electrode materials, thereby enhancing the overall energy density of the capacitor. Consequently, 1 M KOH was selected as the electrolyte for this study.

All electrochemical tests were performed using a CHI660E electrochemical workstation in 1 M KOH with a three-electrode cell system. Hg/HgO was used as the reference electrode. CV tests were performed in the voltage range of −0.6–0.6 V with the scan rate set to 10–200 mV s-1. Based on the analysis of CV test results, distinct redox reactions of the electrode material were observed within the potential range of −0.4 to 0.1 V. Therefore, GCD tests were conducted in the same potential window (−0.4∼0.1 V) with current densities ranging from 0.5 to 5 A/g. The frequency of EIS measurements was limited to 0.01 Hz-100 kHz, and the voltage amplitude at the open loop was 5 mV of voltage amplitude. The asymmetric supercapacitor was fabricated from CMPCD-2 composite material. CMPCD-2 was used as the positive electrode, AC as the negative electrode, and 1 M KOH solution as the electrolyte. The negative electrode was prepared in the same way as the counter electrode in the three-electrode battery. The mass ratio of the positive electrode to the negative electrode is about 1:2 to ensure that the positive and negative electrodes have the same capacity. The CV and GCD tests of the hybrid supercapacitor were also performed in the CHI660E electrochemical workstation. The CV tests were performed in the potential range of 0–2.1 V with a scan rate of 10–200 mV s-1. The GCD tests were performed at current densities ranging from 2 to 5 A/g.

The morphology and structure of the electrode materials have a significant effect on their electrochemical performance, and the formation of the microstructure during the hydrothermal synthesis process depends on the ratio of Cu/Mn atomic concentration in the film preparation, as observed by SEM. The different microstructures of the materials have different adsorption capacities for ions, which in turn affects their electrochemical performances. SEM images of CMP at different magnifications clearly reveal the correlation between the composition of Cu/Mn and the surface morphology.

When the ratio of copper ions in the sample is 0 (CMP-0), the SEM images are shown in Fig. 1 a-b, and the activated material is a rod-like structure with a length of about 62.44 μm and a width of about 33.50 μm. With the continuous doping of copper ions, the morphology of the activated material remains as a flower-like spherical structure when the ratio of copper ions is 0.25, 0.5, and 0.75 (CMP-0.25, CMP-0.5, CMP-0.75). The morphologies of the actives were maintained as flower-like spheres (Fig. 1c-h). The flower-like stacking density of CMP-0.25 was significantly higher than that of CMP-0.5 and CMP-0.75, but CMP-0.5 had the smallest size diameter of about 23.81 μm, which was significantly smaller than the size diameters of CMP-0.5 and CMP-0.75 (Fig. 1c), while retaining a certain degree of flower-like stacking density (Fig. 1c-h). 0.75 (53.33 μm, 85.19 μm). The smaller size diameter provides CMP-0.5 with a larger specific surface area, which effectively improves the adsorption and desorption of ions in the electrolyte and produces a larger specific capacitance, which is the same as that in the subsequent electrochemical test results. At the ratio of copper ions of 1, the activated substance morphology was characterized by rhombic structure, which resulted in the lack of adsorption sites for ions on CMP-1.

Spartina alterniflora-derived carbon dots (CDs) were doped into CMP-X, and it was observed by SEM electron microscopy that the doping of CDs could effectively increase the stacking density of CMP-0.5 without affecting the diameter of the original CMP-0.5 size (Fig. 2 ).

In the samples doped with CDs, a significant increase in the stacking density of the material's nanosheets can be clearly observed after CDs doping. This is attributed to the abundant functional groups in the CDs, which are adsorbed onto the surface of the copper-manganese nanosheets, providing active sites for further growth of the nanosheets [27]. As the concentration of CDs in the material increases, the spatial density of these active sites also rises, leading to more compact and random growth of the nanosheet structures. Consequently, the addition of CDs promotes nanosheet stacking, although a small amount of doping does not affect the thickness of the nanosheets. However, as the doping of CDs continues, the thickness of the nanosheets gradually decreases. This reduction in thickness ensures the structural stability of the material. On the other hand, excessive doping of CDs causes the nanosheets to collapse and densely stack again. In summary, the optimal morphology is achieved at a CDs doping amount of 2 mg, which provides a richer mesoporous structure and enhances the electrochemical performance [28]. As mentioned above, when the doping amount was 1 mg (Fig. 2a,b) or 3 mg (Fig. 2e,f) (CMPCD-1, CMPCD-3), it resulted in the overdense stacking of CMP-0.5, and could not maintain the original obvious flower-like spherical structure, but when the doping amount was 2 mg (CMPCD-2), the obvious flower-like structure was still observed, and this effectively The electrochemical energy storage performance of CMP-0.5 was greatly improved.

To obtain the structural information of the materials, X-ray diffraction (XRD) tests were carried out. The XRD of CMP-X samples with different Cu/Mn ion ratios are shown in Fig. 3 a, in which both CMP-0 and CMP-1 are monometallic phosphates with no other element doping (112),(020),(111) at crystal surface [29]; and the characteristic diffraction peaks of CMP-1 at 23.8°, 36.7°, 38.3°, 47.1°, 55.3°, 56.7°, 58.2°, 58.7°, 63.7°, 64.0° belong to the Cu3(PO4)2 at (111),(021),(020),(113),(−212),(1–21),(230),(311),(−301),(−3–21) at crystal surface [30].When the two elements were doped with each other (CMP-0.25, CMP-0.5, CMP-0.75), the diffraction peaks of Mn3(PO4)2 and Cu3(PO4)2 existed in the XRD, and the diffraction peaks were more sharp, which indicated that the synthesized Cu-Mn phosphates had good crystallinity, which could help to increase the cycling life of the electrode materials. The XRD spectra of the CDs-doped samples have more obvious diffraction peaks at 26.2°, 42.2°, and 59° than that of CMP-0.5, and the diffraction peaks here are the crystalline surfaces of carbon at (002), (100) and (103) [31,32], This suggests that Spartina alterniflora-derived CDs was effectively doped into CMP-0.5 (Fig. 3b). From Fig. 3a and Fig. 3b, it is shown that when the materials are CMP-0 and CMP-1, the XRD peaks shift to the left, which may be caused by the doping of K + during the hydrothermal synthesis process. However, when CDs are doped, no significant XRD peak shift is observed.

According to the HRTEM analysis of the samples (Fig. 4 ), the lattice spacing of CMP-X materials significantly increased, which is consistent with the leftward shift of XRD peaks caused by K + doping, further confirming the successful incorporation of K + into the material. Additionally, analysis of the SAED results (Fig. 4) revealed that when the material contains only a single element, the SAED images of CMP-0 and CMP-1 exhibit a dot array pattern, indicating that both materials possess a single-crystal structure. In contrast, the SAED images of the other samples display distinct diffraction rings, suggesting that these samples have a polycrystalline structure.

Observing the XPS spectra of the prepared samples, it can be found that (Fig. 3c d), CMP-0 and CMP-1 samples lacked the binding peaks of Cu and Mn elements respectively, which is the same as the expected results in the study. With the decrease of the proportion of Mn, the intensity of Mn binding peaks decreases, and the intensity of Cu binding peaks increases. When the CDs doping amount was 2 mg, the intensity of the O binding peaks was significantly higher than that of other samples.

Subsequently, the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis of the elements and their valence states in CMP-0.5 and CMPCD-2 were performed, and the results are shown in Fig. 5 a-d. The characteristic peaks of four elements, namely, Cu 2p, Mn 2p, P 2p, and O 1 s, were observed in the main XPS spectral lines. In the Cu 2p spectrum, the two main binding peaks of Cu 2p3/2 and Cu 2p1/2 are located at 935.3 eV and 955.2 eV, respectively, and a group of strong satellite peaks of Cu2+ at around 943.4 eV are observed at 939.8 eV, 942.7 eV and 944.7 eV [33,34], 963.2 eV, and 959.4 eV are also strong satellite peaks of Cu2+; 641.6 eV, 653.6 eV are the Mn 2p3/2 and Mn 2p1/2 in the Mn 2p spectrum, respectively, and the satellite peaks existed at 647.9 eV are the characteristic satellite peaks of MnO, which indicates that the elemental Mn exists as Mn2+ [35]; In the P 2p spectrum, 133.4 eV and 134.7 eV are the two main binding peaks of P 2p3/2 and P 2p1/2, respectively, and the binding peak of phosphorus is located at 133.4 eV, which indicates that the elemental phosphorus exists in the form of metal phosphate in the material [20]; in the O 1 s spectrum, there are two peaks located at 531.3 eV and 533.0 eV, which are represented by the phosphate (P-O) [12,20,36] and in the -OH group of the oxygen [13]. The XPS results of the CDs-doped samples showed that the intensities of the characteristic peaks in Cu 2p, Mn 2p, P 2p, and O 1 s as well as the energy levels did not change significantly (Fig. 5e-i). Analyzing the C1 s, the characteristic peaks at 286.4 eV, 288.7 eV are all exhibited [16].The K 2p3/2 and K 2p1/2 characteristic peaks of potassium ions at 292.4 eV and 295.1 eV, respectively, indicate that there is some doping of potassium ions during the synthesis of the material.

The specific surface area of the CMP-X and CMPCD-Y electrode was determined by a BET (Brunauer - Emmett - Teller) surface area analyzer using the principle of nitrogen adsorption. Fig. 6 shows the N2 isotherms and pore size distributions of the CMP-X and CMPCD samples, obvious type IV adsorption isotherm, which can be regarded as the H4 hysteresis loop isotherm according to the classification of IUPAC, which indicates that there is no obvious saturated adsorption plateau in the material, and the pore structure is very irregular. Fig. 6c,d shows the pore size distributions of the CMP-X and CMPCD samples, and the percentage of the smaller pore sizes in the CMP-X sample increases gradually with the increase of the concentration of Cu2+; whereas, the CDs-doped CMPCD samples have smaller pore sizes; and the CDs-doped CMPCD samples are smaller. CMPCD samples, the pore size distribution was more concentrated at 2 mg of CDs doping.

Different materials form different morphology and structures, active sites, and diameters, which play an important role in the electrochemical properties of the materials. The electrochemical performance of copper-manganese phosphate (CMP-X) electrodes was analyzed and compared in a 1 M KOH electrolyte using a three-electrode structure with a Hg/HgO electrode as the reference electrode and a platinum electrode as the counter electrode.

The CV curves of the CMP-X electrode at a fixed scan rate of 100 mV s-1 in the potential range of −0.6–0.6 V are shown in Fig. 7 a. Compared with the electrode materials with different Cu2+ concentrations, when Cu2+:Mn2+=1:1 (CMP-0.5), the large current area under the CV curve showed good electrochemical performance, and the CV curves at different scanning speeds (10–200 mV s-1) and the CV curves of the other materials at different scanning speeds were further determined as shown in Fig. 7b, Figure S1. As the scanning speed of the CV curves gradually increased, the redox peaks of CMP-0.5 were shifted to a certain extent, which was attributed to the polarization effect of the electrode at large scanning speeds [37]. At the same time, the CV curve test was also performed on the CMPCD electrode, and the results showed that the area of the CV curve increased significantly when the CDs doping amount was 2 mg (Fig. 8 a). The CMPCD electrode also had a more obvious redox peak in the CV curve (Fig. 8b), and it had a sufficiently fast intercalation pseudocapacitance behavior [38], When the scan rate was gradually increased, the redox peaks were also shifted with CMP-0.5. In addition, the electrochemical storage characteristics can be analyzed based on the CV curves at the tandem scan rate, and formula (1) is given below:
(1)
i = a
v
b
where: i is the peak anodic or cathodic current, v is the scanning velocity, and a and b are variable constants. b denotes the rate of the reaction kinetics, which is obtained from a linear fit of Log i to its corresponding Log v plot. Values of b close to 1 indicate that the surface capacitance-controlled behavior is dominant in the Faradic redox process, while values of b close to 0.5 indicate that it exhibits a typical electrochemical energy storage diffusion-controlled process [39]. The b values of the positive and negative peaks of CMP-0.5 are 0.66 and 0.61, respectively (Fig. 7f), while the b values of the positive and negative peaks of CMPCD-2 are 0.68 and 0.57, respectively (Fig. 8f), which indicates that the charge storage behavior in CMP-0.5 and CMPCD-2 is dominated by the diffusion-control of surface charge [40,41]. The energy storage performance of the electrode material was tested by constant-current charging and discharging (GCD). According to the formula (2), the capacitance of CMP-0.5 is 227 F/g at 0.5 A/g.
(2)
C m
=
I
Δ
t
m
Δ
V

Where Cm is the specific capacitance, I is the current, Δt is the discharge time, m is the mass of the activated material, and ΔV is the discharge voltage. The charge-discharge curves of different materials of CMP-X at a current density of 0.5 A/g are shown in Fig. 7c, and the charging and discharging times of the electrode materials were significantly increased when doped with CDs (Fig. 8c), and the specific capacitance of the electrode materials at 0.5 A/g was 240 F/g, 277 F/g and 262 F/g for CMPCD-1, CMPCD-2 and CMPCD-3, respectively. Table 1 compares the specific capacitance of the CMPCD-2 electrode with previously reported materials, demonstrating its exceptional electrochemical performance.

It is shown that the specific capacitance of CDs doping effectively increases the charging and discharging time of the electrode material, indicating that CDs doping effectively improves the energy storage performance of the original CMP-0.5, and the best performance is achieved when the doping amount is 2 mg. When CMP-0.5 is doped with CDs, the stacking density of CMP-0.5 increases. This increase in stacking density results in CMPCD-Y having a more abundant mesoporous structure compared to CMP-0.5. However, when the doping amount is 1 mg (Fig. 2a, b) or 3 mg (Fig. 2e, f) (CMPCD-1, CMPCD-3), it leads to high-density stacking of CMP-0.5, causing the loss of its original distinct flower-like spherical structure. In contrast, when the doping amount is 2 mg (CMPCD-2), a clear flower-like structure is still observed. The relatively regular flower-like structure of CMPCD-2 results in a higher proportion of mesopores compared to other samples, thereby effectively enhancing the electrochemical energy storage performance of CMP-0.5 [50,51].

The Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) was performed to understand the effect of the material on the charge transfer rate during the charge storage process, so the EIS measurements were carried out in the frequency range of 0.01 Hz to 10 kHz at the electrodes, and the equivalent circuits are shown in Fig. 7e, Fig. 8e. The series resistance (Rs) and charge transfer resistance (Rct) of CMP-X and CMPCD-Y are shown in Table 2 , and it can be seen that when there is Cu2+ doping, the material has a more obvious reduction, and the Rs of sample CMP-1 is as low as 0.98 Ω. The results show that copper doping can effectively reduce the series resistance of the material, which can effectively improve the performance of electrodes and extend the service life. The results show that Cu doping can effectively reduce the series resistance of the material, which can effectively improve the performance of the electrode and prolong its service life; however, when Cu2+ is doped, the material changes from a regular rod structure to a flower-like ball structure, which leads to an increase in Rct. When CDs doping, the Rs of the materials are increased, which may be due to the CDs-induced dense stacking of the materials, but when the CDs doping amount is 2 mg, the Rct resistance of the materials is significantly reduced, which is due to the better water-solubility and electrochemical properties of the CDs to enhance the contact between the materials and the electrolyte solution, so that the ions can realize the rapid diffusion.

The performance of hybrid supercapacitors with asymmetric devices was investigated by using CMPCD-2 as the positive electrode, activated carbon as the negative electrode, and 1 M KOH solution as the electrolyte. The CV curves of the asymmetric CMPCD-2//AC devices were plotted at different maximum voltages (1.2 V∼2.1 V) in Fig. 9 b, and it can be seen that the CV curves do not have obvious polarization as the voltage is increased, and 2.1 V was chosen as the optimal voltage. Fig. 9c shows the CV curves at different scanning speeds, in which the redox peaks are not significantly shifted and the shape of the curves is consistent, which verifies the excellent rate capability and good reversibility. The charging and discharging curves at different current densities (2 A/g∼5 A/g) show that (Fig. 9d) with the increase of current density, the electrodes are more prone to polarization during the charging process, and the specific capacitance is 28.7 F/g at 2 A/g. The maximum energy and power densities of the CMPCD-2//AC asymmetric capacitor were 17.8 Wh kg-1 and 5.25 kW kg-1(Fig. 9f).

The CMPCD-2 composite was synthesized via a 120 °C hydrothermal method. Its flower-like spherical structure was characterized by XRD, SEM, HRTEM and XPS, while electrochemical analyses (CV/GCD/EIS) demonstrated superior performance in 1 M KOH electrolyte, achieving a specific capacitance of 277 F/g at 0.5 A/g. The hierarchical morphology facilitated electrolyte infiltration and ion adsorption through abundant active sites. Furthermore, the assembled asymmetric supercapacitor (ASC) exhibited 28.7 F/g at 2 A/g with an energy density of 17.8 Wh kg-1 at 2.1 kW kg-1 power density. This work provides a cost-effective strategy for designing high-performance supercapacitor electrodes, advancing sustainable energy storage technologies.

Xueshi Zhang: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qingyi He: Methodology. Xiang Qi: Writing – review & editing, Supervision. Xinyi Wang: Software, Resources. Yingshu Hao: Visualization, Validation, Project administration. Xianru Zhang: Investigation, Data curation. Minchao Zhang: Supervision. Xinshan Song: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xin Cao: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No.2232023D-14,2232019D3–21); the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No.52470178,52000024,51909034).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:10.1016/j.electacta.2025.145964.
